title: Bag of Words
concept: The Bag of Words model represents text by counting how often each word appears, ignoring grammar and word order completely but capturing basic topic indicators.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need to convert text into numerical representations for AI models?

options.text: It makes the text look prettier.
Computers need numbers, not raw words, to perform calculations.
It makes the text much longer and harder to read.

exercises.explain: Computers cannot read text like humans do, so we must convert words into numbers (like counts) so algorithms can process them.

cards.title: Reading by Counting
cards.body: How can a computer understand what a whole book is about without reading it line by line? It turns the book into a big pile of scrambled words and starts counting!

cards.title: What is Bag of Words?
cards.body: The Bag of Words model is a simple way to turn text into numbers. It acts like a counter: it makes a list of all unique words and counts how many times each word shows up in a document, ignoring where they are placed.

cards.title: The Recipe Bag
cards.body: Imagine cutting up a recipe book and throwing all the individual words into a shopping bag. You lose the sentences, but if you pull out the words 'flour', 'sugar', and 'bake' twenty times, you can easily guess it was a baking recipe!

cards.title: Counting Cats
cards.body:
For the sentence: "cat likes milk and cat likes fish", the Bag of Words count is:
• cat: 2
• likes: 2
• milk: 1
• and: 1
• fish: 1

The grammar is gone, but we know it's about a cat!

cards.title: Order Doesn't Matter
cards.body: People think Bag of Words is smart enough to understand word order. It isn't! "Not good, bad" and "Not bad, good" get the exact same word counts, even though they mean opposite things.

cards.title: Sentence vs. Bag
cards.body: See how a sentence loses its structure and order when it is transformed into a bag of word counts.

exercises.prompt: What does a Bag of Words model care about the most?

options.text: How many times each word appears
The order of the words in a text

exercises.explain: Bag of Words is all about tracking the frequency (or count) of words, ignoring their order and grammar.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The Bag of Words model cannot tell the difference between "Cats eat fish" and "Fish eat cats".
exercises.explain: True! Bag of Words loses all word order. "No rain, sunshine" and "Rain, no sunshine" look identical to the model.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the explanation of the Bag of Words model:
exercises.sentence: The Bag of Words model counts word frequency but completely ignores sentence structure and [blank].
exercises.explain: Bag of Words completely throws away grammar and word order, focusing only on how frequently words appear.

exercises.prompt: A human writes: "The happy puppy chased the red ball down the street." Which system output represents a Bag of Words analysis of this sentence?

options.text: System A: "The happy puppy chased the red ball down the street."
System B: "ball: 1, chased: 1, happy: 1, puppy: 1, red: 1, street: 1, the: 3"
System C: "A young dog ran after a colorful toy outdoors."

exercises.explain: System B is generated by a Bag of Words model because it scrambled the word order and just listed the words and their counts. System A kept the grammatical structure.

exercises.prompt: If we feed the sentences "Dog bites man" and "Man bites dog" into a Bag of Words model, how does it see them?

options.text: It easily separates them because it understands grammar rules.
It keeps track of the order of words in each sentence.
It treats them as identical because they contain the exact same words and counts.
It ignores both sentences because they are too short.

exercises.explain: The Bag of Words model only counts how many times words appear. It does not understand the structure or meaning of grammar, so "Dog bites man" and "Man bites dog" look exactly the same to it!
